David Scholz

Graph Theorist, Computer Scientist, Software Engineer

Email: david.scholz@pgrepds.io

PGP Key: C598 A64F B172 98B6 F31D B7F8 62EA 240E FF87 32AE

Web: https://pgrepds.io

About Me

I am a Ph.D. student at the University of Cologne at the Institute for Computer Science.

My research interests lie in chromatic- and structural graph theory, mostly on the structure of graphs with certain forbidden induced subgraphs. In particular I am interested in the chi-boundedness of graphs, what structural relationships make a graph chi-bounded and how an efficient algorithm for constructing an optimal colouring can be derived from this relationships.

Research Interests

Structural- and Chromatic Graph Theory

chi-binding functions, forbidden induced subgraphs, hereditary graph properties

Algorithm Design

graph colouring, graph generation, graph recognition, complexity theory

Computer-Aided Mathematics

automated conjecture-making and automated reasoning

Personal Interests

Research takes up most of my life. In the little time available, I am interested in the latest technologies used in software development, in current programming languages and in almost all new achievements from other fields of mathematics, computer science and physics. I am increasingly involved in IT security and cryptography in particular and I currently spend most of my free time dealing with topics from these interesting areas.

I was a passionate endurance athlete for a long time and still do a variety of sports, such as running, swimming and weight training. Besides that, I currently like to occupy myself with the philosophy of Immanuel Kant, the literature of J.R.R. Tolkien and with playing chess.

I am fascinated by the endless exactness, flawlessness and pure beauty of mathematics and its consequent manifestation in computer science. Especially the apparent simplicity of graph theory, which can become so arbitrarily complex, captivates me every day and drives me into a perfectionist obsession to solve its problems and I could not be more grateful for the opportunity to realise myself in science, and no matter how strenuous the path may become, it is a quite wonderful one.
